coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodot-modified FeOCl for photo-assisted Fenton reaction featuring synergistic in-situ H2O2 production and activation
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jun
Zhang, Gong
Ji, Qinghua
Lan, Huachun
Qu, Jiuhui
Liu, Huijuan
coredata.dc:description:
Achieving efficient production and activation of H2O2 is a promising strategy to enhance the efficiency of heterogeneous Fenton reactions. For this purpose, an ideal heterogeneous Fenton platform was constructed though chemical embedding of carbon nanodots (CDots) onto the surface of iron oxychloride (FeOCl/CDots). Experimental results and theoretical calculations confirmed that CDots acted as the reactive sites for highly effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes in the FeOCl substrate, whereby the O2 molecules in solution could be synchronously reduced to H2O2 via a two-electron pathway (O2 →
O2H → H2O2) on the CDots. Under visible light irradiation, the concentration of H2O2 accumulated to ∼337.2 μmol L−1 in the FeOCl/CDots system, which was almost ∼14 times higher than that using FeOCl alone. Moreover, the FeOCl substrate with abundant Fe2+ could directly capture and activate the H2O2 to produce abundant hydroxyl radical (
OH), thereby effectively avoiding the H2O2 mass transfer limitation encountered in traditional heterogeneous Fenton reactions. In experiments with a typical organic contaminant (p-chlorophenol), the oxidation rate and mineralization efficiency in this Fenton system were ∼4 and ∼6 times higher than that achieved using FeOCl alone.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FeOCl/CDots
In-situ H2O2
Generation and activation
Photocatalytic Fenton
p-Chlorophenol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Financial Supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51722811 , 51738013 ) and Guangxi ‘Bagui Scholar’ Construction Project (Grant/Award Number: 2016A10 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM and (b) EDS mapping images of FeOCl/CDots; (c) XRD patterns, (d–f) High-resolution XPS spectra and (g) Raman spectra of CDots, FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (h) ESR spectra of CDots, FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (i) The structure models of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots.

(a) DRS spectra, (b) I-T curves and (c) PL spectra of CDots, FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (d) LSV curves of FeOCl/CDots at different rotation speeds; (e) Koutecky-Levuch plots of FeOCl/CDots at different potentials; (f) Free energy diagrams of H2O2 generation pathways on FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (g) Band structure diagram for FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (h) CV curves of FeOCl/CDots under different conditions; (i) In-situ generated H2O2 contents for CDots, FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots.

(a) Schematic activation process of additional H2O2; (b) Schematic of separate active sites for production and activation of H2O2; (c) Schematic of coupled active sites for production and activation of H2O2; (d–e) DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra recorded with FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (f) Photocatalytic-Fenton degradation of 4-CP by prepared CDots, FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots; (g) Effect of different initial pH; (h) Effect of different initial TCA concentrations; (i) Degradation of five different organic pollutants by FeOCl/CDots; (j) Recycling runs of 4-CP removal for FeOCl/CDots.

(a) Schematic photocatalytic-Fenton degradation of 4-CP mechanism for FeOCl/CDots; (b) Quenching experiment of 4-CP removal for FeOCl/CDots; (c) In-situ generated H2O2 contents for FeOCl/CDots in the presence of TEMPOL or N2; PL spectra of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots in the presence of (d) Fe(II)/EDTA, (e) TEMPOL or (f) N2.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot-modified FeOCl for photo-assisted Fenton reaction featuring synergistic in-situ H2O2 production and activation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Water and Ecology, State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control, School of Environment, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Water and Ecology, State Key Joint Laboratory of Environment Simulation and Pollution Control, School of Environment, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Science and Technology, Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100085, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Drinking Water Science and Technology, Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100085, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Achieving efficient production and activation of H2O2 is a promising strategy to enhance the efficiency of heterogeneous Fenton reactions. For this purpose, an ideal heterogeneous Fenton platform was constructed though chemical embedding of carbon nanodots (CDots) onto the surface of iron oxychloride (FeOCl/CDots). Experimental results and theoretical calculations confirmed that CDots acted as the reactive sites for highly effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes in the FeOCl substrate, whereby the O2 molecules in solution could be synchronously reduced to H2O2 via a two-electron pathway (O2 →
O2H → H2O2) on the CDots. Under visible light irradiation, the concentration of H2O2 accumulated to ∼337.2 μmol L−1 in the FeOCl/CDots system, which was almost ∼14 times higher than that using FeOCl alone. Moreover, the FeOCl substrate with abundant Fe2+ could directly capture and activate the H2O2 to produce abundant hydroxyl radical (
OH), thereby effectively avoiding the H2O2 mass transfer limitation encountered in traditional heterogeneous Fenton reactions. In experiments with a typical organic contaminant (p-chlorophenol), the oxidation rate and mineralization efficiency in this Fenton system were ∼4 and ∼6 times higher than that achieved using FeOCl alone.

ce:section.ce:para: Heterogeneous Fenton technology has been shown to effectively remove refractory organics in water due to its strong oxidation capacity [1–4]. However, its wide application has been primarily restricted by the need for H2O2 supplementation [5,6]. In addition to excessive cost from the aspects of storage and transportation, the conventional H2O2 production process commonly depends on the anthraquinone oxidation/reduction process, which is detrimental to the environment due to its high energy consumption [7]. Therefore, the in-situ production of sufficient H2O2 during the heterogeneous Fenton process should be a promising approach for environmental application of this technology.

In contrast to green technologies based on an electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) process, the photocatalytic conversion of O2 to H2O2 is considered to be more sustainable in terms of energy consumption [8]. However, the low utilization efficiency of photo-induced electrons in the two-electron transfer pathway has been the main bottleneck for photocatalytic H2O2 production, and the embedding of an appropriate co-catalyst onto the photocatalyst should be a promising approach to improve the efficiency [9,10]. In view of their highly tunable properties and chemical stability under different conditions, carbon materials of various types have been applied as ideal candidates in a variety of H2O2 synthesis systems [11–14]. Recent studies have found that carbon atoms adjacent to several oxygen functional groups (COOH and COC) are active sites for ORR via the two-electron pathway [15]. Therefore, to achieve excellent ORR performance, the selected carbon material must possess a wealth of the above functional groups, and expose the maximum number of active sites to accelerate the reaction process. Among the many forms of carbon materials, carbon nanodots (CDots) show great promise as alternative cocatalysts for the photocatalytic synthesis of H2O2 due to their special zero-dimensional structure. This particular structure possesses a larger specific surface area compared to carbon materials with other morphologies, which could provide adequate reaction sites for ORR [16–19].

Furthermore, the efficient activation and application of H2O2 is another decisive parameter for the heterogeneous Fenton process [20–22]. In traditional heterogeneous Fenton reactions, the H2O2 must always go through a complex and slow mass transfer process before it can be activated to form hydroxyl radical (
OH), which seriously reduces the utilization rate of H2O2 and lowers the reaction efficiency [23,24]. To overcome this drawback, we took the view that the fixation of active sites (CDots) for in-situ H2O2 production on the surface of a photocatalyst substrate with a catalytic H2O2 activation function would be a particularly effective method to solve the mass transfer limitation problem. In this way, the photocatalyst not only could endlessly provide electrons for CDots for in-situ generation of abundant H2O2, but the formed H2O2 also could be efficiently decomposed by the base photocatalyst to produce ample
OH in-situ. The construction of a photocatalytic system featuring the synergistic effect of in-situ H2O2 production and activation should be a promising pathway to the effective application of heterogeneous Fenton reactions.

Iron oxychloride (FeOCl), a ternary layered n-type photocatalyst, possesses a rich array of landing points for embedding CDots due to its unique two-dimensional structure with large specific surface area [25–28]. Meanwhile, FeOCl also has excellent photoelectric conversion performance to provide abundant electrons, and its suitable band structure also guarantees smooth electron transfer from FeOCl to the CDots, achieving continuous and efficient in-situ H2O2 production. In addition, the abundant Fe2+ in FeOCl guarantees ultrafast H2O2 activation to produce ample
OH. In view that the abundance of Fe2+ in its layered structure, it has been reported that high concentration of
OH can be achieved when using FeOCl nanosheets as activator, which is almost 2–4 times higher than the other Fe-containing activators [27,29]. In contrast to the Fenton-like processes using other activators, FeOCl-based Fenton-like processes even exhibit superior
OH production performance and organic pollutants degradation properties at unfavorable pH range [25,29]. In this work, we innovatively prepared a carbon nanodot modified FeOCl (FeOCl/CDots) heterogeneous photocatalytic-Fenton platform via a simple synthesis process. We found that the chemical bonding of CDots not only measurably improved the photocatalytic activity via extending light absorption and facilitating photocarrier transfer, but also could effectively promote the generation of abundant H2O2 in-situ by a two-electron pathway (O2 →
O2H → H2O2). In addition, the formed H2O2 would be directly captured by the FeOCl substrate and rapidly activated by Fe2+ in FeOCl to produce abundant
OH, which effectively avoids the complex and slow mass transfer process of H2O2 in traditional heterogeneous Fenton reactions and thus ensures the smooth running of the photocatalytic-Fenton reaction. This photocatalytic-Fenton system showed an excellent oxidation rate and mineralization efficiency for p-chlorophenol (a typical organic contaminant) [30]. Our study may be of interest to both materials scientists and those working in the area of the wastewater remediation.

All chemicals were used directly without further treatment. The CDots were synthesized by a previously described electrochemical method [31]. The FeOCl nanosheet photocatalyst was prepared via calcining FeCl3·6H2O at 230 °C for 2.5 h with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1. To obtain the FeOCl/CDots composites, the FeCl3·6H2O and CDots (mass ratio was 20:1) were first mixed well together by grinding, and then the mixture was calcined under the same conditions (Fig. S1).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU-8020, Hitachi, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100 F, Japan) were used to determine the morphology and phase structure of the catalysts. X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Bruker D8, Germany) were measured to identify the phase composition of the catalysts. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, VG ESCALAB210, USA), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR, Nicolet iS50, USA) and Raman (Via-Reflex, Renishaw, UK) spectroscopy were applied to explore the surface states and elemental composition of the catalysts. UV–vis diffuse reflection (DRS) spectra were collected on a U-3900 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fluorescence (PL) spectra were obtained by a luminescence spectrometer (F-4500, Hitachi, Japan). Electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker model ESR E500 spectrometer by using the spin-trap reagent DMPO (5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide, Sigma Chemical Co., USA).

In a sealed quartz reactor, 0.1 g of catalyst was added to a 100 mL p-chlorophenol (4-CP) aqueous solution (5 mg L−1). The mixture was fully stirred for 30 min in the dark to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium. After that, the suspension was irradiated with visible light, and a given volume of reaction solution was sampled at regular intervals and filtered by a 0.45 μm membrane. The visible light source was a 150 W Xenon lamp with a UV-420 filter (100 mW cm−2). The pH of solution was controlled and adjusted by 2 mol L−1 NaOH and H2SO4. It is worth noting that the pH adjustment was conducted after the addition of catalysts. The stability and repeatability of photocatalysis was measured by repeating experiments six times under the same conditions. The scavenger quenching experiments were conducted by adding different scavengers, including tertiary butanol (TBA, 0.5 mmol L−1, Sigma, 99.5 %) for
OH, Fe(II)-EDTA for H2O2 and 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy (TEMPOL) for
O2– [31].

The 4-CP concentration was determined by a high-performance liquid chromatography system (HPLC, Alliance e2695, Waters, USA) equipped with a 4.6 × 250 mm, 5 μm Venusil HILIC column. Ultrapure water (35 %) and acetonitrile (65 %) were used as mobile phase (v/v) with a flow rate of 1.00 mL min–1. The mineralization degrees of organic pollutants were detected via a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-L, Shimadzu, Japan). The released concentration of Fe was confirmed using an inductively coupled plasma source mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, Thermo Fisher iCAP Q, USA). The degradation products of 4-CP were identified with a liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS) system (QTRAP 5500, AB SCIEX, USA). The mobile phase was a mixture of ultrapure water (35 %) and acetonitrile (65 %) with a flow rate of 0.25 mL min–1, and the retention time was 30 min. The generated H2O2 content was determined by the iodometric method, with the modification that the potassium iodide solution (KI) and ammonium molybdate solution (H8Mo2N2O7) were added in advance [5]. The formation of
OH was detected via the fluorescence method with benzoic acid (BA) as a probe molecule [32]. The photoelectrochemical analyses were performed in a typical three-electrode configuration. The tests included chronoamperometry (I–T curve), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), Mott-Schottky curve measurement, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV).

The first principles calculations in the framework of density functional theory, including structural, electronic performances, were carried out based on the Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package known as CASTEP. The exchange-correlation functional under the generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with norm-conserving pseudopotentials and Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof functional were adopted to describe the electron-electron interaction. An energy cutoff of 750 eV was used and a k-point sampling set of 7 × 7 × 1 was tested to be converged. A force tolerance of 0.01 eV Å−1, energy tolerance of 5.0 × 10−7 eV per atom and maximum displacement of 5.0 × 10−4 Å were considered. The vacuum space along the z direction was set to be 15 Å, which was enough to avoid interaction between the two neighboring images. The Grimme method for DFT-D correction was considered for all calculations.

For FeOCl/CDots systems, the FeOCl(001) surface was built, the bottom three atomic layers were fixed and the three atomic layers were relaxed, the cut-off surface was O atom. Moreover, a C cluster (C12) was loaded on the FeOCl(001) surface for FeOCl(001)/CDots system. For reactions, the intermediates were adsorbed on the surfaces of FeOCl(001) and FeOCl(001)/CDots.

Adsorption energy ΔE of A group on the surface of substrates was defined as:
ΔE = E *A – (E * + E A)
where *A and * denote the adsorption of A group on substrates and the bare substrates, E A denotes the energy of A group.

Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) of each chemical reaction is calculated by:
ΔG = ΔE + ΔZPE − TΔS + ΔG U
where E, ZPE, T and S denote the calculated total energy, zero-point energy, temperature and entropy, respectively. ΔG U = –eU (U was the potential measured against normal hydrogen electrode). Here, T = 300 K was considered.

The prepared CDots showed irregular spherical shapes with particle diameters ranging from 2.5 to 5.5 nm (Fig. S2a). Both FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots presented a thin 2D nanosheet-like structure, indicating that the embedding of CDots would not affect the phase structure of FeOCl (Fig. S2b and c). Fig. S3 further confirms the 2D nanosheet-like structure of FeOCl/CDots, with the elements of Fe, O, Cl and C as the components of FeOCl/CDots. HRTEM indicated that the CDots were evenly distributed over the FeOCl surface, and the lattice fringes of CDots could be observed clearly (Figs. S2d and 1 a). The EDS mapping further confirmed the uniform distribution of CDots and that the as-prepared composites only included FeOCl and CDots without other substances (Fig. 1 b).

The diffraction peak at 26.5° in the XRD pattern of CDots corresponded well to the (002) crystal plane of graphite (Fig. 1c) [18]. In the XRD patterns of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots, the diffraction peaks located at 11.1°, 26.2° and 35.5° were attributed to the (010), (110) and (021) crystal planes of FeOCl (JCPDS file No. 72-0619), respectively (Fig. 1c) [26,27]. The strong diffraction peaks showed that the FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots were highly crystalline [26]. No characteristic diffraction peaks of CDots were observed in the XRD pattern of FeOCl/CDots due to the small content and relatively low diffraction intensity of CDots [31,33]. The diffraction peaks showed slight shift to lower angle after the addition of CDots, suggesting the presence of a strong interaction between CDots and FeOCl (Fig. S4a) [18,31,33]. This strong interaction would likely be a source of tensile stress in the FeOCl/CDots, which could cause lattice anisotropy, resulting in the diffraction peaks shifting to a lower angle [33]. The intimate contact and strong bonds between FeOCl and CDots were beneficial to electron transfer during photo-excitation [31,33].

We further detected the surface chemical states of FeOCl/CDots by XPS, FT-IR, Raman and ESR analyses. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. S3c) showed the presence of Fe, O, Cl and C in the FeOCl/CDots sample. Two distinct peaks at 711.9 and 725.8 eV belonged to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, along with satellite peaks at 719.6 and 733.5 eV (Fig. 1d) [34]. In addition, the peak intensities of FeOCl/CDots showed a relative decrease, which was caused by the formation of FeC bonds and thus shielding of X-rays. After CDots were embedded, the peak at 532.3 eV significantly increased in intensity, implying the formation of CO and CO bonds (Fig. 1e) [18]. Another peak at 530.8 eV was ascribed to FeO bonds [25]. Two strong peaks at 198.9 and 200.6 eV were identified as the Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 of FeCl bonds (Fig. S4b) [27]. The broad peak in the C 1s spectrum was resolved into three peaks centered at 284.8, 285.4 and 289.3 eV, corresponding to CC, CO, CO and FeC bonds, respectively (Fig. 1f) [17,31].

The FT-IR analysis could clearly reveal the nature of the surface functional groups in FeOCl/CDots. The peak at 1639.7 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra of CDots and FeOCl/CDots was associated with the stretching vibrations of CO and CO bonds (Fig. S4c) [31]. In the FT-IR spectra of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots, the characteristic peaks at 1598.5 and 811.1 cm−1 were suggested to be the stretching modes of FeCl and FeO bonds, respectively [28]. Meanwhile, the new peak at 1436.1 cm−1 in FeOCl/CDots was suspected to be due to FeC bonds. Raman spectra further confirmed the successful combination of FeOCl and CDots via detecting the formation of characteristic bonds (Fig. 1g). Two characteristic peaks at 1350.8 and 1600.1 cm−1 in the Raman spectra of CDots and FeOCl/CDots corresponded to the d-band (the vibrations of disordered carbon atoms) and the G-band (the sp2 bonded carbon atoms), respectively [26,35]. For FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots, four peaks appeared at around 219.2, 282.7, 392.6 and 1291.5 cm−1, corresponding to FeCl and FeO stretching modes, respectively [26]. A small peak at 341.9 cm−1 showed good correspondence to the FeC stretching modes. In addition, the ESR spectrum of FeOCl/CDots showed a significant paramagnetic signal with the g value of 2.01, attributed to the unpaired electrons on oxygen (Fig. 1h) [36], which was caused by the formation of CO and CO bonds. This result indicated that the embedding of CDots would change the surface state of FeOCl and form a small number of oxygen vacancies. According to the existing literature [37], the introduction of oxygen vacancies is beneficial for enhancing oxygen adsorption, which is conducive to the combination of electrons and oxygen to form reactive oxygen species (ROSs).

The above analyses show that CO, CO and FeC bonds formed after embedding the CDots into FeOCl, and the corresponding structure models of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots are shown in Figs. 1i and S4d. The models show that the CDots bonded to oxygen and iron, and thus the CDots were anchored on the surface of FeOCl. The formed CO, CO and FeC bonds improved the charge transfer from FeOCl to CDots, resulting in reduced electron–hole recombination and enhanced reactive performance.

The embedding of CDots also could markedly increase the light absorption for the semiconductor substrate over the entire spectrum. A strong, broad and continuous absorption band appeared in the DRS spectrum of CDots, indicating that the composites containing CDots would effectively utilize solar energy (Fig. 2 a) [18]. As expected, the FeOCl/CDots sample showed good light absorption across the entire examined region after incorporating the CDots into the FeOCl matrix. More available charge would be generated under this illumination, resulting in higher photocatalytic activity. Compared with FeOCl, the FeOCl/CDots exhibited higher photo-current response (Fig. 2b). The current density in the FeOCl/CDots system was ∼2.1 times higher than that of FeOCl, meaning more electrons were generated in FeOCl/CDots. There was a sharp photo-current spike in the I-T curve of FeOCl, attributed to electron accumulation and rapid electron-hole recombination [38], while the transient peaks entirely disappeared after embedding CDots, suggesting effective electron transfer in FeOCl/CDots. The EIS Nyquist plots further confirmed a much higher rate of interfacial charge transfer in FeOCl/CDots (Fig. S5a). The decreased semicircle radius in the Nyquist plot indicated more efficient separation and transport of charge carriers [39]. Both FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots showed an emission peak at 630.8 nm in PL spectra under the same conditions (Fig. 2c). The stronger peak intensity for FeOCl was attributed to the fast recombination of photogenerated charges [40]. This peak was significantly weakened after modification with CDots because of the charge space separation [16,31]. We also compared the time–resolved PL spectra of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots, and the latter showed the longest lifetime, which indicated efficient electron–hole separation (Fig. S5b).

The electrons concentrated in the CDots could be used to reduce dissolved oxygen to form H2O2 by a two-electron ORR pathway. The dynamic process of oxygen reduction on FeOCl/CDots was investigated via the rotating disk voltammetry [41]. The obtained LSV curves showed that the limiting diffusion current density gradually increased with increasing rotation speed (Fig. 2d). The Koutecky-Levuch (K–L) equation was employed to describe the relationship between the current density and rotation speed at different potentials, and the corresponding K–L plots exhibited good linearity (Fig. 2e). The overall number of electrons transferred (n) was quantitatively estimated according to the slope of the K–L plots, and the n values were in range of 2.4–2.7 at different potentials. Thus, oxygen reduction on FeOCl/CDots was a mixed process of two-electron and four-electron pathways, and the two-electron transfer was dominant. The significantly high current density value and two-electron ORR pathway explained the high activity and selectivity of photocatalytic H2O2 production on FeOCl/CDots.

The thermodynamic and kinetic properties of O2 reduction to H2O2 on FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots were further compared via DFT calculations with FeOCl(001) and FeOCl(001)/CDots as models (Figs. 2f and S6). On FeOCl(001), the direct adsorption of oxygen was exothermic by −1.31 eV. Embedding CDots could effectively enhance the oxygen adsorption on FeOCl(001)/CDots (−2.13 eV). Then, for the formation of HO2
, the energy change on FeOCl(001)/CDots was −1.35 eV, which was significantly more negative than that on FeOCl(001) (−0.39 eV), indicating higher catalytic activity for HO2
formation on FeOCl(001)/CDots. Meanwhile, the highly spontaneous transformation from *O2 to HO2
on FeOCl(001)/CDots resulted in little
O2 − formation, and the *O2 preferentially formed HO2
. For subsequent H2O2 generation processes, the energy barrier from HO2
to H2O2 on FeOCl(001)/CDots (−1.59 eV) was also much lower than that on FeOCl(001) (−0.22 eV). Embedding CDots made H2O2 formation easier, which is in good accordance with the above experimental results.

The above analyses confirmed the feasibility of the two-electron ORR pathway on FeOCl/CDots. However, whether the electrons could transfer from FeOCl to CDots would depend on the energy band structure of FeOCl/CDots. A suitable band gap position would guarantee the smooth transfer of electrons form FeOCl to CDots. The VB energies (Ev) of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots were obtained by UPS spectra, giving an Ev value of 5.92 eV (Fig. S7a). The band gaps (Eg) of FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots were determined to be 1.81 eV via Tauc plots (Fig. S7b). The CB gap (Ec) was thus identified as 4.11 eV from Ev − Eg. The values of Ec, Ev and Eg could be converted to electrochemical energy potentials (volts) via the reference standard of 0 V versus RHE which equals −4.44 eV versus evac (vacuum level) [18]. The flat band potentials (Vfb) of FeOCl (1.19 eV) and FeOCl/CDots (1.20 eV) were measured via Mott-Schottky plots, which were consistent with the above analyses (Fig. S7c). Fig. 2g lists the CB and VB positions of FeOCl, and it is clear that the CB position is negative enough to fully guarantee effective transfer of electrons from FeOCl to CDots, and that the electrons gathered in CDots have enough potential energy to reduce O2 and form H2O2 thermodynamically.

We further used CV curves to compare the ORR properties of as-prepared FeOCl/CDots with or without light illumination (Fig. 2h) [13,42]. The area of the CV curve was relatively small in dark conditions, and little or no oxygen reduction peak could be observed over the whole range. By contrast, the current density was remarkably enhanced under visible light irradiation, and a strong oxygen reduction peak appeared at ∼1.12 V (vs. RHE). This indicated that the ORR on FeOCl/CDots only occurred with visible light excitation; namely, the photogenerated electrons from FeOCl/CDots were used to reduce O2 to form H2O2. In contrast, CV curves of pure FeOCl shows negligible changes after the infusion of O2 compared to being under N2 condition (Fig. S8), indicating the FeOCl has ineffective ORR performance Based on the above analyses, we measured and compared the actual concentrations of H2O2 produced for FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots. The H2O2 generation in the FeOCl/CDots system (∼337.2 μmol L−1) was much larger than that of FeOCl (∼23.7 μmol L−1) or CDots (∼1.8 μmol L−1) separately in 3 h of reaction (Fig. 2i). The H2O2 contents gradually accumulated with reaction time (Fig. S9). The combination of FeOCl and CDots effectively increased the concentration of H2O2, which was mainly caused by the synergistic effects between them [6]. We thus confirmed that the photogenerated electrons transferred from FeOCl to CDots, and that H2O2 formed on the surface of the CDots.

In traditional heterogeneous Fenton processes (Fig. 3 a and b), whether the additional H2O2 transfers to the surface activation sites or the in-situ generated H2O2 transfers from the production site to the surface of activation sites, it would need to go through a complex and slow mass transfer process. By contrast, the processes for production and activation of H2O2 were all conducted on the surface of FeOCl/CDots without a slow mass transfer process (Fig. 3c), which facilitated efficient removal of organics in wastewater. The generated ROSs were detected by ESR analyses, and the results showed that abundant
OH was generated, and little
O2 − appeared in the FeOCl/CDots system (Fig. 3d and e). In contrast,
O2 − was the dominant ROS in the FeOCl system, and the amount of
OH formed was negligible. This demonstrated that the embedding of CDots was the crucial factor for
OH generation, and the CDots led to main ROSs transforming from
O2 − to
OH. It is worth noting that a signal of DMPO−
O2H appeared in the FeOCl/CDots system but not in FeOCl, because
O2H was the critical transition state for
OH generation in FeOCl/CDots. PL spectra further indicated that the amount of
OH in FeOCl/CDots was far greater than that in the FeOCl system, and the content of
OH accumulated over the reaction time in the FeOCl/CDots system (Fig. S10).

Due to the abundant
OH present in the FeOCl/CDots system, it correspondingly showed the highest photocatalytic-Fenton performance for 4-CP degradation (∼90.1 %) (Fig. 3f). The calculated apparent reaction rate constant (k obs value) for FeOCl/CDots was 0.012 min−1, which was 4 times higher than that in the FeOCl system (k obs =0.003 min−1) (Fig. S11a). The percentage variation of released Cl− showed a similar trend to the degradation curve of 4-CP (Fig. S11b). The mineralization efficiency of 4-CP was as much as ∼41.5 %, much higher than that reported in the relevant literature (Fig. S11c) [43,44]. In addition, most of the H2O2 generated in-situ was utilized for the degradation and mineralization of 4-CP, and the decomposition ratio of H2O2 was ∼80.1 % in the end (Fig. S11d).

Previous reports showed that solution pH had a significant effect on homogeneous Fenton reactivity, and the optimum value was generally ∼3.0 [45]. Unlike the homogeneous Fenton reaction, the heterogeneous Fenton process usually adapts well to a higher pH environment. To verify this point, we explored four different pH conditions, and the results showed that the maximal k obs value appeared for initial pH = 5.0 (Fig. 3g), due to the higher generation and decomposition efficiency of H2O2 at this pH (Figs. S12 and S14a–b). In comparison to the work condition in range of weak acid and neutral conditions, the slightly reduced 4-CP degradation rates can be observed in strong acid and weak base conditions. This was due to the fact that the FeOCl could not stably exist in strong acid condition [29], which certainly will decrease rates of H2O2 production and activation. Meanwhile, weak base condition was easily causing hydroxyl complexation on FeOCl surface, so that number of active sites were reduced for H2O2 activation. Studies have shown that the unique coordination environment of Fe in FeOCl is relatively unaffected by changes in solution pH, which was unlike Fe2+ in homogeneous Fenton reactions, whose coordination is highly pH-dependent (i.e., more hydroxyl complexation at higher pH) [6,21,25]. This wide pH range is beneficial to the practical application of the FeOCl/CDots system.

To investigate effect of dissolved O2, we have compared the in-situ H2O2 production contents and corresponding 4-CP degradation efficiencies in four different dissolved O2 concentrations (Fig. S13). The concentrations of H2O2 exhibit a positive relationship to aqueous O2 concentration. Accordingly, the most effective 4-CP removal performance was obtained at the condition of the highest dissolved O2 concentration. On increasing the initial concentration of 4-CP from 2 to 20 ppm, the removal efficiency gradually decreased, which was most likely caused by the saturation of reactive sites (Fig. 3h). We found a positive correlation between the k obs value and the log of the initial concentration (Fig. S14c–d), further confirming the above hypothesis. Five other typical organic pollutants, including rhodamine B (RhB), bisphenol A (BPA), Phenol, tetracycline (TC) and atrazine (ATZ), were investigated to further evaluate the reactivity and universality of the FeOCl/CDots heterogenous Fenton system (Fig. 3i). The different contaminants were almost completely removed, and the mineralization efficiencies also reached relatively high levels.

The as-prepared FeOCl/CDots showed acceptable stability and reusability. A small reduction in photocatalytic-Fenton performance was observed after 6 cycles of use. The H2O2 production capacity of FeOCl/CDots slightly decreased, and the 4-CP removal efficiency was only reduced from the initial value of 90.7 % to a final value of 80.2 % (Figs. S15a and 3 j). We also investigated the released Fe content after each cycle, and the result showed that the release of Fe gradually diminished with cycling (Fig. S15b). Continuous release of Fe was the main cause of the performance degradation of FeOCl/CDots [25]. It was clear that the phase structure and optical properties of FeOCl/CDots had not changed after 6 cycles, indicating the excellent performance stability of FeOCl/CDots (Fig. S15c–d).

Combining the ROS analyses and experimental results, we could identify that
OH played the key role in 4-CP removal, which also was the main reason that the FeOCl/CDots system showed excellent 4-CP removal performance, much higher than that of FeOCl. We investigated the 4-CP degradation pathway and the intermediate products formed in the process (Fig. S16). Similar to previous studies, the generated
OH would effectively attack and replace the Cl located in para-position, or add in the ortho-position of 4-CP [46]. The formed hydroquinone and 4-Chlorocatechol would be further oxidized to benzoquinone and a diacid. Most of the 4-CP and intermediates could be completely broken down into CO2, H2O and Cl− as the reaction progressed. The
OH radical was essential during the whole process for 4-CP removal.

The above results showed that abundant H2O2 was generated in-situ on FeOCl/CDots, and the formed H2O2 was further rapidly activated to produce abundant
OH for 4-CP removal. There were two possible processes for H2O2 activation, including Fe2+/Fe3+ redox cycling and photogenerated electrons. To reveal which process occurred, we first investigated the valence state changes of Fe in FeOCl/CDots by XPS analysis (Fig. S17a). Some Fe2+ existed in the used FeOCl/CDots, and subsequent addition of H2O2 could oxidize the Fe2+ to convert it to Fe3+ in the dark. This showed that the Fe2+/Fe3+ cycle could be efficiently completed via oxidation by H2O2 and reduction by photogenerated electrons. In addition, adding additional H2O2 had almost no effect on the trend of LSV and EIS curves of FeOCl/CDots, whether the current density or impedance, demonstrating that there was no direct electron transfer between FeOCl/CDots and H2O2 (Fig. S17b–c) [47]. The results showed that the H2O2 generated in-situ was activated via the Fe2+/Fe3+ cycle instead of direct photogenerated electron transfer. In other words, the photogenerated electrons would reduce Fe3+ to form Fe2+, and then the H2O2 was activated by Fe2+, rather than the electrons directly acting on H2O2.

In theory, the photocatalysts themselves are readily oxidized or reduced during light irradiation process. Without exception, certain amount of Fe3+ on the surface of FeOCl is easily converted to Fe2+ by some portion of as-excited electrons, which can be observed in previous study [29]. Meanwhile, the remaining photogenerated electrons are easily captured by active or recombination sites on surface of FeOCl. In addition to the recombining electrons, the other photogenerated electrons will further transfer to CDots and combine with adsorbed O2 molecule to form H2O2, which has been verified in ORR experimental results.

Based on the above results and literature data, a probable photocatalytic-Fenton destruction process of contaminant was proposed, as shown in Fig. 4 a. First, the FeOCl was excited by visible light to generate electron-hole pairs, and the electrons in the CB of FeOCl were quickly transferred to CDots via the coupling interface between FeOCl and CDots. Secondly, the accumulated electrons in CDots were used to reduce the adsorbed oxygen to efficiently form H2O2. Then the generated H2O2 was rapidly reduced by Fe2+ in FeOCl to produce abundant
OH for 4-CP degradation. However, based on result of ICP-MS analysis, we founded that a small amount of FeOCl will be dissolved during the H2O2 activation process, with a total Fe concentration of only ∼50 μg L−1, which has the negligible contribution toward 4-CP degradation process. Finally, the remaining holes in the VB of FeOCl may also partly contribute to 4-CP degradation, which was achieved by direct oxidation. Quenching experiments also showed that
OH was the main ROS for 4-CP degradation by FeOCl/CDots (Fig. 4b). Meanwhile,
O2 − was not an important species for 4-CP removal, and direct oxidation by holes made up a very small percentage in the 4-CP degradation process. The H2O2 contents in the FeOCl/CDots system were detected using N2 (for O2) and TEMPOL (for
O2 −) as quenchers, respectively, which was in agreement with the above analyses (Fig. 4c).

We further used fluorescence quenching experiments to verify the above photocatalytic-Fenton processes. It was clear that the PL intensity of
OH in both FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots was significantly decreased after adding Fe(II)/EDTA, which was used as a trapping agent for H2O2 (Fig. 4d). This confirmed that the
OH was generated by way of H2O2 →
OH. In addition, after quenching the
O2 − by TEMPOL, the PL emission peak in FeOCl almost disappeared, but this had no significant effect on the performance of FeOCl/CDots (Fig. 4e). This result suggested that
O2 − was the transition state for H2O2 production in FeOCl but not in the FeOCl/CDots system, which was consistent with the ESR spectra and DFT calculations. We further verified the above inference by removing dissolved oxygen in the reaction solution (Fig. 4f). This showed that the PL intensity of
OH in both the FeOCl and FeOCl/CDots systems was drastically lowered in the absence of oxygen. These results indicated that the O2 was mainly converted into
O2 − in FeOCl, and part of the
O2 − would be further converted into H2O2. By contrast, the O2 tended to be effectively reduced to H2O2 by a two-electron pathway (O2 →
O2H → H2O2) in the FeOCl/CDots system without production of significant amounts of
O2 −.

In summary, the FeOCl/CDots heterogeneous photocatalytic-Fenton platform was successfully prepared by a simple synthesis process. The embedding of CDots not only measurably improved the photocatalytic activity via extending light absorption and facilitating photocarrier transfer, but could effectively promote the FeOCl/CDots to generate abundant H2O2 in-situ by a two-electron pathway (O2 →
O2H → H2O2). Meanwhile, due to the intimate contact between FeOCl and CDots, the formed H2O2 could be directly captured by the FeOCl substrate and rapidly activated by Fe2+ in FeOCl to produce abundant
OH, which effectively avoids the complex and slow mass transfer process of H2O2 occurring in traditional heterogeneous Fenton reactions. The typical refractory organic pollutant 4-CP could be removed and mineralized effectively in this system.

Jun Zhang and Gong Zhang designed experiments; Jun Zhang and Qinghua Ji carried out experiments; Jun Zhang and Jiuhui Qu analyzed experimental results; Huachun Lan analyzed characterization data and developed analysis tools; Jun Zhang and Huijuan Liu wrote the manuscript.
